text: Harsh fluorescent lights of a restaurant kitchen. The sound of clattering plates fills the air.

name: MainChar (Internal)
text: 'Fifteen years. I’m still waiting, you idiot. Some 'knight' you turned out to be.'
You sigh, wiping down the last table. You’ve traded your 'kingdom' for a cramped apartment and a job as a waitress in a city that never stops moving.
Your friend’s face has faded into a blur of dual-colored eyes and a stray mole you can’t quite place anymore.

name: Boss
text: Good work today, [Player Name]. Don’t let the door hit you on the way out.

name: MainChar
text: Yeah, yeah. Try not to let the place burn down without me, Boss.
You wave him off with a tired smirk, but as you step into the cool evening air, the playful mask slips. Your feet ache, and the silence of the walk home feels heavier than usual.
As you reach your front door, something catches the light. A slip of cream-colored paper is tucked into your weathered mailbox.
You bring it inside, kicking off your shoes and dropping your bag with a heavy thud.
The envelope is pristine—too expensive for this neighborhood. As you break the seal, the faint, cloying scent of dried roses drifts into the room.

name: letter
text: Dear [Player Name], Time has a cruel way of burying the truth, hasn't it? I know of the 'Knight' you lost all those years ago. If you wish to see him again, to see the man he has become.
I invite you to play. Seek out Oletus Manor, and your questions will be answered. — The Baron
Your grip tightens, crinkling the expensive parchment. Your heart thumps against your ribs, a frantic, rhythmic demand for action.
Though there was a doubt lingering in your mind, the fact that they knew of your friend was proof enough to quell that doubt in the bud.
You look around your empty, quiet kitchen. The doubt is there, lingering like a shadow, but the resolve in your chest is colder and harder.
It didn't cost you much, just to participate in what ever 'game' the Baron of the manor ought you to play.
You didn’t rush. Experience had taught you that haste only leads to forgotten keys or burnt toast.
You spent the next few days tying up loose ends—telling your boss you needed an indefinite leave for 'urgent family matters.'

name: MainChar (Internal)
text: A family crisis. Not a total lie, I suppose, if I still have any family left to find. My employer looked at me as if I were a cracked saucer, but he granted the time.

name: MainChar (Internal)
text: Better he thinks I’m mourning a distant cousin than chasing a phantom from a five-year-old’s memory.
You sent a few vague letters to acquaintances, ensuring no one would file a missing person’s report the moment you stepped off the grid.
You packed heavy, prepared for anything from a weekend stay to a long-term siege.
The car ride was long and quiet, the city lights fading into the skeletal silhouettes of a dense forest.

When the taxi finally lurched to a halt, the driver didn't even wait for a tip. He dumped your luggage on the gravel and sped away, the roar of his engine swallowed by the oppressive silence of the valley.

name: MainChar (Internal)
text: Charming. Nothing says 'welcome' like a terrified local and a mile-long walk in sensible shoes.
The Manor loomed through the gloom, a jagged crown of stone and ivy. You hauled your trunk toward the iron gates, which stood tall and forbidding.
Before you could even lift the heavy brass knocker, the great oak door groaned open, spilling a sliver of warm, flickering candlelight onto the porch.
A man stood there, clad in the stiff, black livery of a high-house butler. His face was a pale mask of professional indifference, though his eyes seemed to weigh your very soul.

name: Butler
text: Welcome, dear guest. We have been expecting your arrival. Please... step out of the damp.

name: MainChar
text: Expecting me? I assume the Baron’s post is more reliable than the city’s, then. I trust my letter of invitation reached the correct hands?
You kept your voice steady, though the sheer scale of the foyer made you feel like the orphan child you once were.
The butler offered a thin, ghost of a smile. Precisely. If you would follow me, the others are already gathered in the drawing room. Your luggage will be seen to.
He gestured with a gloved hand toward a set of double doors, from which the scent of pipe tobacco and burning cedar drifted.

name: MainChar (Internal)
text: Other guests.' So I’m not the only soul the Baron has lured into his web. Perhaps... he might even be here too.
The drawing room is a cavern of velvet and shadows, lit only by the dying amber glow of a massive hearth.
The air is thick with the smell of coal smoke and the metallic tang of something you can’t quite name.

name: MainChar (Internal)
text: So, this is the ‘company’ I’m keeping. A gambler and a stage-actor. The Baron certainly has a type.
Your eyes drift to the first man, lounging against the marble mantle. He wears a heavy, dirt-stained coat that looks out of place in such a grand room.
His skin is pale, save for a jagged, dark scar that maps across his face—a permanent reminder of a life lived underground.
He doesn't look up as you enter; his focus is entirely on the heavy iron coin he flips.
Clink. Catch. Clink. Catch.

name: MainChar (Internal)
text: He looks like he’s counting down the seconds until something explodes. Or until he can rob the place blind.
Then, your gaze shifts to the man standing opposite him.
He is the polar opposite—back straight as a pike, clad in an outfit that mimics a knight of old, though the fabric is a bit too theatrical for a real battlefield.
His hand rests with white-knuckled intensity on the hilt of a prop sword.
He isn't looking at the fire; he’s staring into the darkness of the room as if expecting a dragon to emerge from the curtains.

name: MainChar (Internal)
text: And this one... he looks like he hasn’t blinked since the turn of the century. Is he guarding the fire, or is he afraid it'll go out?
The silence in the room is heavy, broken only by the rhythmic clink of the coin and the crackle of the fire.
You stand there for a moment, waiting for the hospitality that clearly isn't coming.

name: MainChar
text: Quite the welcoming committee. Does the silence come with the room, or do I have to pay extra for a 'hello'?
The man by the fire doesn't move, but the one leaning against the mantle finally slows his coin-tossing.
He looks at you with a sharp, weary gaze that seems to calculate the cost of your coat and the weight of your luggage in seconds.

name: Norton
text: Names are expensive in a place like this,' he says, his voice raspy and stripped of any warmth.'

name: Norton
text: But if you’re going to be taking up space, you might as well call me Norton Campbell. Not that it’ll do you much good once the games start.
He doesn't offer anything else, turning his attention back to his iron coin as if you’ve already become part of the wallpaper.
The man standing guard by the fire finally shifts. He doesn't look at Norton; his focus is entirely on you, though his gaze feels strangely distant, as if he’s looking at a figure in a history book.

name: Richard
text: A knight does not leave a traveler unacknowledged. I am Richard Sterling.
He speaks the name with a peculiar, stiff pride, as if he’s still getting used to the way it tastes.

name: Richard
text: I suggest you keep close to the light. This house has a habit of swallowing those who wander.

name: MainChar
text: Norton and Richard. Right. Well, since we’re sharing... I’m [Player Name]. I hope we can all get along.
Your legs start to feel tired, you look around and find a few places where you could sit down.
